var.explanation: Declares a function-scoped (or global) variable. Its value can be reassigned, and it's hoisted to the top of its scope. Prefer let/const in modern code.

let.explanation: Declares a block-scoped variable whose value can be reassigned later. This is the standard way to declare a variable you plan to change.

const.explanation: Declares a block-scoped variable that cannot be reassigned after it's set. The value itself can still be mutated if it's an object or array.

function.explanation: Defines a reusable block of code that can take inputs (parameters) and optionally return a value.

=>.explanation: Arrow function syntax — a shorter way to write a function. Arrow functions don't have their own 'this', they inherit it from the surrounding scope.
=>.example:
const add = (a, b) => a + b;
console.log(add(2, 3)); // 5

if.explanation: Runs a block of code only when a condition is true.

else.explanation: Runs a block of code when the preceding if (or else if) condition was false.

else if.explanation: Checks another condition when the previous if was false. You can chain several of these together.

for.explanation: A loop that repeats a block of code a set number of times, using a counter variable (start; condition; step).

for...of.explanation: Loops over the values of an iterable (like an array or string), one at a time.

for...in.explanation: Loops over the enumerable property names (keys) of an object.

while.explanation: Repeats a block of code as long as a condition stays true. The condition is checked before each iteration.

do...while.explanation: Like a while loop, but the code runs once before the condition is checked, so it always executes at least once.

switch.explanation: Compares a value against several possible cases and runs the matching block, instead of writing many if/else if statements.

case.explanation: Defines one branch inside a switch statement to match against.

break.explanation: Immediately exits the nearest loop or switch statement.

continue.explanation: Skips the rest of the current loop iteration and moves on to the next one.

return.explanation: Exits a function and optionally sends a value back to whoever called it.

try.explanation: Marks a block of code to 'try' running, so that if it throws an error, you can catch and handle it instead of crashing.

catch.explanation: Runs when the matching try block throws an error, giving you access to the error object.

finally.explanation: Runs after the try/catch, whether or not an error occurred — useful for cleanup code.

throw.explanation: Raises an error, immediately stopping normal execution so it can be caught by a try/catch (or crash the program if uncaught).

class.explanation: Defines a blueprint for creating objects with shared properties and methods.

extends.explanation: Makes one class inherit properties and methods from another (parent) class.

constructor.explanation: A special method inside a class that runs automatically when a new object is created from it, used to set up initial values.

this.explanation: Refers to the current object instance a method is running on. Its value depends on how the function was called (and arrow functions inherit it from the surrounding scope).

super.explanation: Calls the parent class's constructor or methods from within a subclass.

new.explanation: Creates a new instance (object) from a class or constructor function.

static.explanation: Defines a property or method that belongs to the class itself, not to individual instances of it.

import.explanation: Brings in code (functions, classes, values) exported from another file or module.

export.explanation: Makes a function, class, or value from the current file available to be imported elsewhere.

async.explanation: Marks a function as asynchronous, meaning it always returns a Promise and can use 'await' inside it to pause for other async operations.

await.explanation: Pauses an async function until a Promise settles, then gives you its resolved value (or throws if it rejected).

typeof.explanation: Returns a string describing the type of a value, like 'string', 'number', 'boolean', 'object', or 'function'.

instanceof.explanation: Checks whether an object was created from a specific class (or its subclasses).

null.explanation: Represents the intentional absence of any value — you explicitly set something to 'nothing'.
null.example: let selected = null; // no item selected yet

undefined.explanation: The default value of a variable that has been declared but not yet assigned a value.

spread.explanation: The '...' syntax expands an array or object into individual elements — useful for copying, merging, or passing many arguments at once.

destructuring.explanation: A shorthand syntax for pulling values out of arrays or objects into their own variables.
destructuring.example:
const { name, age } = person;
const [first, second] = [10, 20];

template literal.explanation: A string written with backticks that lets you embed expressions directly using ${...}, and supports multi-line text.

Promise.explanation: An object representing a value that will be available later (after an asynchronous operation finishes successfully or fails).

??.explanation: The nullish coalescing operator — returns the right-hand value only if the left-hand value is null or undefined (unlike ||, it keeps 0 and '' as-is).

?..explanation: Optional chaining — safely accesses a nested property without throwing if something along the way is null or undefined.
?..example: console.log(user?.address?.city); // undefined instead of a crash
